Unified Customer Profile Service Cloud Web Tracking
Case Study · B2B SaaS · Enterprise

Maropost

Role — Associate UX Designer
Duration — May 2023 – Oct 2024 · 17 months
Team — PM · Engineers · QA
Figma FigJam Miro Jira Agile / Scrum End-to-end

Led UX design across three interconnected enterprise products — Web Tracking, Service Cloud, and Unified Customer Profile — for a B2B SaaS marketing platform competing with Salesforce, Zendesk, and Freshdesk.

Products Designed
3 Enterprise
Products
Feature Adoption Lift
+30%
Design System
40+ New
Components
Dev Handoff Speed
20% Faster
Context

An enterprise suite with four products, one designer.

Maropost is a B2B marketing automation platform — think Salesforce Marketing Cloud for mid-market companies. It runs four products (Commerce, Marketing, Service, and Retail) used by enterprise clients to run their entire customer operations.

I joined as the Associate UX Designer embedded across Web Tracking, Service Cloud, and Unified Customer Profile. Each product had its own PM and engineering team, its own user base, and its own pile of legacy debt. I was the sole designer responsible for end-to-end UX — from discovery and research through to dev handoff and QA.

I also introduced and championed a structured design→development→QA handoff process that hadn't existed before — embedding designers into implementation and testing cycles for the first time.

The Challenge
Three separate products. Three separate user types. All data-heavy, enterprise-grade, and previously designed without a cohesive system or structured handoff process. Low feature adoption despite strong engineering quality.
My Approach
User interviews · Usability testing · Competitive audits (Zendesk, Freshdesk, GA4, Mixpanel) · FigJam journey mapping · High-fidelity interactive prototyping · Component-first design system thinking.
Process Introduced
I pushed for and established a designer-in-the-loop QA process — something the team hadn't prioritised before. This meant designers reviewed builds before release, catching UX regressions early and significantly reducing back-and-forth.
Product 01 / 03

Unified Customer Profile One customer.
One truth.

Maropost had four separate products with four separate user databases. Enterprise clients using multiple products had no unified view of their end-customers — support agents, marketers, and account managers were all working blind.

Cross-product Data Merge 4 DBs → 1 View Dashboard Design
User DashboardUnified profile view — open rate, lifetime value, orders, and full history timeline across all 4 Maropost products in one place.
Information ArchitectureData model showing how Marketing, Commerce, Service, and Neto databases merge into a single Unified Customer Profile.
🔍
The Problem
Four products. Four silos. Zero visibility.
  • Maropost had 4 products — Commerce, Marketing, Service, Retail — each with its own isolated database
  • Enterprise clients using 2+ products had no way to see a single customer's full activity across platforms
  • Support agents couldn't see if a customer also had a Marketing or Commerce history when handling a ticket
  • Marketers were targeting the wrong audiences because purchase data lived in Commerce, not Marketing
  • This created massive context-switching overhead and trust issues with enterprise clients
⚙️
My Approach
Map the data. Design the merge. Test the trust.
  • Ran stakeholder interviews with PMs from all 4 product teams to understand what data existed and what would be technically feasible to merge
  • Mapped the full customer data model in FigJam — what fields lived where, what overlapped, what was unique
  • Conducted usability testing with 3 internal power users (account managers, support agents) to validate which data points mattered most in a unified view
  • Did competitive research on Salesforce 360 and HubSpot's contact records to benchmark information hierarchy
  • Iterated through 3 rounds of wireframes before moving to high-fidelity — key debate was how to surface cross-product activity without overwhelming the view
✦
The Solution
A single, powerful profile. Built for context.
  • Designed a unified customer dashboard that stitched together all 4 product databases into a single profile view
  • Timeline-first layout showing all customer touchpoints — purchases, support tickets, marketing interactions — in chronological order
  • Smart identity resolution panel: if a customer email matched across products, it auto-merged the records
  • Role-aware information architecture: support agents saw ticket history first; marketers saw campaign and purchase behaviour first
  • Built 12+ new design system components specifically for this feature — data tables, timeline events, activity rings, identity cards
Explore the Designs
Product 03 / 03

Web Tracking Every click.
Every dollar.

Enterprise marketers sending email and SMS campaigns had no way to see what happened after a recipient clicked through. Revenue attribution was a black box. Web Tracking was designed to change that.

+30% Feature Adoption Revenue Attribution Funnel Analytics
Main DashboardOverview of website page views and orders from email/SMS campaigns, with funnel chart showing conversions, abandonments, and recoveries.
Funnels ViewDedicated funnel analytics showing total sessions, conversions, abandonments, and recoveries with a stacked daily bar chart.
Orders ViewRevenue attribution dashboard — total revenue, orders, items, and customers with a multi-line timeline of order statuses over time.
📉
The Problem
Revenue was being generated. Nobody could prove it.
  • Maropost clients sent millions of email and SMS campaigns but had zero visibility into what happened after a link click
  • Marketing teams couldn't attribute website revenue, orders, or funnel completions back to specific campaigns
  • This made it nearly impossible to justify campaign spend to stakeholders or optimise messaging
  • Existing analytics tools (GA, Mixpanel) weren't integrated — data lived in disconnected systems
  • Low adoption of the feature when it initially launched because the data was there but unreadable
🔬
My Approach
Start with what marketers actually need to decide.
  • Interviewed 3 power-user marketers to map their existing workflow — what they checked in GA, what they still couldn't answer
  • Ran a competitive audit of GA4, Klaviyo's attribution reports, and Mixpanel's funnel views
  • Mapped the decision tree: "If I'm a marketer reading this dashboard, what do I need to know first, second, third?"
  • Key insight: Users didn't want more data — they wanted the 4 numbers that told them if the campaign worked
  • Prototyped 2 competing information hierarchies and tested with internal users before committing
📊
The Solution
Dense data. Zero confusion.
  • Redesigned the Web Tracking dashboard with a "hero metrics" layout — Total Revenue, Orders, Funnel Completions, and Redirects surfaced immediately at the top
  • Designed a per-campaign breakdown table with sortable columns — marketers could drill from overview to campaign-level in one click
  • Added a funnel visualisation showing drop-off at each stage from email click → landing → checkout → purchase
  • Introduced date-range comparison (this period vs last period) with delta indicators — something competitors had but Maropost lacked
  • Adoption jumped 30% within the quarter after redesign launched

Product 02 / 03

Service Cloud Support that
knows the customer.

A customer support platform handling tickets from email, social media, and web forms — but agents worked blind. No customer history. No context. No way for managers to track team performance. The product that Ankit is most proud of.

Multi-source Ticketing Agent + Manager Views Customer History
New Ticket Details PageRedesigned ticket view with conversation thread, internal notes, activity log, and customer info panel. Major revamp with extensive research and iteration.
Merge TicketsAgents can merge similar tickets into one primary ticket — for cases where a user sends two tickets for the same issue across different channels.
Custom ViewsAgents personalise their ticket queue by selecting sections they need most and saving filtered views as named shortcuts for faster daily access.
🎫
The Problem
Tickets from everywhere. Context from nowhere.
  • Support tickets came in from email, social media handles, and website contact forms — all landing in different places
  • Agents had no single inbox — they switched between tools to handle different ticket sources
  • When a customer contacted support, agents had zero visibility into their purchase history, previous tickets, or subscription status
  • Managers had no performance dashboard — they couldn't see ticket resolution rates, agent response times, or SLA compliance
  • No categorisation system meant tickets were manually triaged, which was slow and inconsistent
🗺️
My Approach
Two users. Two jobs. One system.
  • Mapped two distinct user journeys: the support agent handling tickets day-to-day, and the manager tracking team-level metrics
  • Ran usability sessions with 2 internal customer success team members to understand their real daily workflow
  • Competitive analysis of Zendesk, Freshdesk, and Intercom — particularly how they handled multi-source inboxes and customer context
  • Key design challenge: how much customer history to show inline without overwhelming the ticket view
  • Ran 3 prototype iterations — tested collapsed vs expanded customer history panel; expanded won decisively
  • Collaborated with engineering to define the data model for cross-product customer history pull (tied to UCP work)
🛠️
The Solution
Every ticket. Full context. Zero switching.
  • Unified inbox combining email, social, and web form tickets — with source tags and smart auto-categorisation
  • Customer history panel that auto-populated when an existing customer was identified — showing their full journey across Maropost products
  • Timeline view for each ticket showing all interactions, status changes, and internal notes chronologically
  • Role-based dashboards: agents saw their queue and SLAs; managers saw team-wide resolution metrics, individual agent performance, and ticket volume trends
  • Designed 16+ new components for this feature — ticket cards, status badges, agent performance widgets, SLA countdown indicators
